stem: A 19-year-old university student meets criteria for ADHD and is motivated to start pharmacotherapy. He lives in a shared dormitory and discloses that last year a roommate crushed and snorted his previous stimulant. You want an agent that is least likely to produce a rapid "high" if it is insufflated or injected. Which stimulant is engineered as an inactive form that must be enzymatically hydrolyzed in the bloodstream before it becomes active, giving it the lowest liability for misuse by snorting or injection?
explanation: Lisdexamfetamine is an inactive prodrug — d-amphetamine covalently bound to L-lysine — that is hydrolyzed by red-blood-cell peptidases to active dextroamphetamine. Because conversion is rate-limited and happens in the blood, crushing and snorting or injecting it does not generate a fast euphoric surge, which blunts its misuse liability by those routes. Dextroamphetamine and Amphetamine mixed salts are already-active amphetamines that can be insufflated or injected for immediate effect, and Methylphenidate is likewise immediately active and misusable this way. All four are controlled drugs in Canada, but only the prodrug specifically defeats the snort/inject route.
focus: prodrug/abuse-liability (lisdexamfetamine) — inactive until hydrolyzed in blood, lowest diversion/insufflation risk among stimulants

source.name: Lisdexamfetamine product monograph — prodrug activation & misuse

stem: An 8-year-old with ADHD has a comorbid chronic tic disorder and marked sleep-onset insomnia. His parents decline controlled stimulants. You want a single non-controlled agent that can help daytime attention while also being sedating enough to give at bedtime to aid sleep onset and reduce tics. Which agent best matches all of these goals?
explanation: Clonidine is a non-selective central α2-adrenergic agonist and the most sedating of the ADHD agents, which is exactly why it is favoured for comorbid tics and sleep-onset insomnia and is often dosed at bedtime. Guanfacine is also a non-stimulant α2 agonist useful for tics, but it is more α2A-selective and therefore less sedating, making it a weaker fit when bedtime sedation is specifically desired. Atomoxetine is non-controlled but is not sedating and has a delayed 2–4 week onset, so it does not address the sleep or tic problem the same way. Methylphenidate is a controlled stimulant that can worsen both tics and insomnia. Both α2 agonists must be tapered to avoid rebound hypertension, but only Clonidine matches the sedation-at-bedtime goal.
focus: alpha-2 agonist for tics/comorbid — clonidine's lower α2A-selectivity and greater sedation favour it over guanfacine for tics + sleep-onset insomnia

source.name: CADDRA/CANMAT ADHD guidance — α2 agonists for tics and sleep

stem: A 30-year-old adult with ADHD and a history of stimulant misuse is started on a non-controlled medication. At a 6-day follow-up she reports no improvement in focus and asks to stop it, saying "it isn't working." She confirms she has been taking it every day as prescribed. Which medication was most likely started — one whose therapeutic benefit characteristically emerges only over about 2 to 4 weeks rather than on the first day?
explanation: Atomoxetine is a non-stimulant selective norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor; because it raises prefrontal norepinephrine and dopamine without the striatal dopamine surge that stimulants produce, its full benefit builds over roughly 2–4 weeks. A lack of effect at 6 days is therefore expected and is not treatment failure — she should continue. Methylphenidate and Amphetamine mixed salts are stimulants with same-day onset (behavioural effect within 30–60 minutes), so no benefit at all after correct dosing would be unusual — and both are controlled, which is a poor fit given her misuse history. Guanfacine is also non-controlled but its benefit emerges over about 1–2 weeks and it produces early sedation/blood-pressure effects; the classic 2–4 week delayed-onset profile in a patient chosen specifically to avoid a controlled agent points to atomoxetine.
focus: atomoxetine onset weeks — delayed 2–4 week benefit of the non-stimulant NRI vs same-day stimulant effect

source.name: Atomoxetine product monograph — delayed onset of effect

stem: During teaching rounds, a pharmacology attending asks the group to identify the first-line ADHD stimulant that works purely by BLOCKING the dopamine and norepinephrine transporters to prevent reuptake — without entering the presynaptic neuron to reverse the transporters and trigger vesicular RELEASE of these transmitters. This release step is the mechanism that distinguishes the other options. Which agent is being described?
explanation: Methylphenidate is a pure reuptake blocker at the dopamine and norepinephrine transporters (DAT/NET); it does not reverse the transporters or drive vesicular release. Dextroamphetamine, Lisdexamfetamine (which liberates dextroamphetamine after hydrolysis), and Amphetamine mixed salts all act primarily as releasers of dopamine and norepinephrine — entering the neuron and reversing transport — in addition to blocking reuptake. This release-versus-block contrast is the classic mechanistic distinction between methylphenidate and the amphetamine-class stimulants.
focus: MPH vs amphetamine mechanism — methylphenidate is a pure DAT/NET reuptake blocker whereas amphetamines are transmitter releasers
